name: Thread-Local Storage Models
summary: Four dominant models for thread-local variables: local-exec (offset known at link, fastest, executables only), initial-exec (resolved at program start, fast, libraries present at start), general-dynamic (per-access dynamic resolution, slowest, dlopen-friendly), local-dynamic (amortised general-dynamic). Compiler picks per access.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Thread-local variables require ABI support. The dominant models:

- **Local-exec**: TLS variable offset known at link time relative to the thread's TLS block. Fastest access but requires linker cooperation; only works for executables, not shared libraries.
- **Initial-exec**: offset resolved at program start via the dynamic linker. Fast access (one indirection) but only works if the library is present at program start.
- **General-dynamic**: offset resolved on each access via `__tls_get_addr`. Slowest but most flexible; works for libraries loaded via `dlopen`.
- **Local-dynamic**: like general-dynamic but amortises the lookup across multiple accesses in the same function.

sections.title: Compiler model selection
sections.content:
The compiler must choose a model per TLS access based on visibility and module kind. Many languages just expose "thread-local" and let the backend pick; a few (C++ with `thread_local`, Rust with `#[thread_local]`) give users manual control.

The choice has real performance implications. Local-exec is essentially free (single instruction); general-dynamic costs a function call per access. For hot-path TLS (per-thread caches, scheduler state), choosing the right model can be the difference between trivial overhead and noticeable cost. Most compilers default conservatively to a model that works in any deployment; performance-critical code overrides explicitly.
